task: I'm building a custom `smithers ui` panel that renders quota across every registered account. Every usage adapter — Claude subscription, Codex ChatGPT, the API-key header probes, and the Google 'none' case — is supposed to hand back ONE normalized object so the table renders the same way regardless of provider. What is that object's shape, what does each field mean, and inside it how do I tell a subscription 'percent' window apart from an API-key request-count window?
notes: A correct answer must describe the UsageReport type: accountLabel, provider, authMode ('subscription' | 'api-key'), source ('oauth' | 'headers' | 'local' | 'none'), windows (UsageWindow[]), optional planType, optional credits {hasCredits, unlimited, balance?}, fetchedAt (ISO-8601), stale (bool), estimate (bool), and optional error. It must explain UsageWindow.unit decides which fields are meaningful: a 'percent' window carries usedPercent (0-100); a 'count' window carries limit/remaining/used; 'estimated' is a locally-derived lower bound. It must NOT invent fields like cost/tokensSpent or claim a single flat number per account.

task: I registered a `kimi` account and ran `smithers usage`. Instead of crashing it printed the account with a 'not supported' note. Which single function routes an account to the right usage adapter by provider, and what does it return for a provider that has no usage surface (so the whole command never throws)?
notes: getAccountUsage(account) switches on account.provider and calls the matching adapter; unsupported providers (e.g. kimi) return a probe with source 'none' and a readable error. Adapters never throw — they degrade to a none report — so one bad account cannot break the table.

task: In `smithers usage` my `anthropic-api` account shows authMode 'api-key' while my `claude-code` account shows 'subscription'. Adapters only return a partial probe (source/windows/etc) — which function wraps that probe into the full report with the account identity, timestamp, and authMode, and what decides whether authMode is 'api-key'?
notes: buildUsageReport(account, probe) stamps accountLabel, provider, fetchedAt, stale:false, and authMode. authMode is 'api-key' when API_KEY_PROVIDERS (from @smithers-orchestrator/accounts) has the provider, otherwise 'subscription'.

task: My `claude-code` account's usage probe keeps returning none. What endpoint does the Claude subscription adapter actually request, and what is special about the User-Agent it must send (the endpoint rate-limits hard without it)?
notes: claudeOauthUsage fetches https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage with Authorization: Bearer <oauth accessToken>, the anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20 header, and a User-Agent that must start with 'claude-code/' (e.g. claude-code/2.0.0) or the endpoint drops you into an aggressive rate-limit bucket. It returns none on missing creds, expired token, 401, or 429.

task: I'm writing code against the Claude subscription usage payload (the same data the in-CLI /usage view shows). Which top-level payload keys does the parser turn into windows, and which numeric field on each block holds the percentage used?
notes: parseClaudeOauthUsage reads p.five_hour ('5h'), p.seven_day ('weekly'), and optionally p.seven_day_opus and p.seven_day_sonnet, each as a 'percent' window. The percentage comes from each block's `utilization` field (NOT `used_percent`, which is Codex); the rollover time comes from `resets_at`.

task: `smithers usage` returns none for my Codex (ChatGPT-subscription) account even though `codex` itself works. What endpoint does the Codex usage adapter call, and which request header identifies the ChatGPT account when reading it?
notes: codexWhamUsage fetches https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/wham/usage with Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>, User-Agent codex-cli, and (when present) the ChatGPT-Account-Id header from the account's credentials. It is the same data the Codex /status view shows and does not spend a turn.

task: The Codex usage payload nests its windows under rate_limits.primary and rate_limits.secondary. In the Codex parser, which block field gives the percent used, which gives the window length, and what is the unit of the reset field?
notes: parseCodexUsage reads rate_limits.primary/secondary (the object may also sit at top level); each block's `used_percent` is the percentage, `window_minutes` maps to a label/id (~300 = 5h, ~10080 = weekly), and `reset_at` is unix SECONDS (multiplied by 1000 to an ISO resetsAt). It also pulls plan_type and credits {has_credits, unlimited, balance}.

task: For an `anthropic-api` (API-key) account, smithers reads live rate-limit headers without burning output tokens. Which Anthropic endpoint does it hit to get the rate-limit header family for free, and which auth header does it send?
notes: anthropicHeaderUsage POSTs to https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages/count_tokens with x-api-key and anthropic-version 2023-06-01; count_tokens returns the anthropic-ratelimit-* header family but produces no output tokens. 401 => none; 429 => still parses the headers it has.

task: I'm parsing Anthropic rate-limit response headers into windows. Which header prefixes does the parser look for, and do I have to convert the reset value into an absolute timestamp myself?
notes: parseAnthropicRateLimitHeaders reads the -limit/-remaining/-reset triples under anthropic-ratelimit-requests, anthropic-ratelimit-tokens, anthropic-ratelimit-input-tokens, and anthropic-ratelimit-output-tokens, producing 'count' windows (used = limit - remaining). Anthropic returns RFC-3339 reset timestamps directly, so no clock math is needed (unlike OpenAI's Go-duration headers).

task: To read OpenAI rate-limit headers for an `openai-api` account, smithers can't use a free listing call because it returns no rate-limit headers. Which endpoint does the OpenAI adapter call instead, and what keeps the cost down to a single output token?
notes: openaiHeaderUsage POSTs a minimal completion to https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions with max_tokens: 1 because GET /v1/models returns no rate-limit headers; only a model request does. It reads x-ratelimit-* headers from the response.

task: OpenAI's x-ratelimit-reset-requests header comes back as something like '6m0s', not a timestamp. How does the OpenAI header parser turn that into the absolute resetsAt on each window, and what would go wrong if I treated '6m0s' as if it were already an ISO time?
notes: A correct answer must say the reset header is a Go-style duration RELATIVE to now, so the parser calls parseDurationSeconds('6m0s') to get seconds and then adds the current time (nowMs) to produce an absolute ISO resetsAt (new Date(nowMs + seconds*1000)). It reads x-ratelimit-limit-requests/-remaining-requests and the tokens pair the same way. Treating it as already-absolute would render a reset time near the unix epoch / wildly wrong. It must NOT claim OpenAI returns RFC-3339 directly (that's Anthropic).

task: Why does `smithers usage` show 'not supported' for my Gemini / Antigravity accounts? Is there a response header I can read to get remaining Google quota right now?
notes: googleUsage returns source 'none': Google (Gemini, Antigravity, Gemini API) exposes no live remaining-quota surface to a personal-login or API-key client — there are no rate-limit response headers, only a 429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED after the wall is hit. The documented future path is a LOCAL estimate against published caps, not yet wired; reporting none is honest rather than inventing a number.

task: Since Google has no live usage API, smithers keeps a table of published quota numbers to estimate against. What is the exported table (and its lookup helper), and what dimension are the caps expressed in?
notes: PUBLISHED_CAPS maps a tier id (e.g. 'code-assist-free', 'ai-pro', 'ai-ultra', 'gemini-api-free') to { label, requestsPerDay, rpm? }; publishedCapForTier(tier) looks one up and returns undefined for unknown tiers. Caps are documented requests-per-day, kept as data because the numbers move.

task: Where does smithers find the Claude Code OAuth token it needs to probe subscription usage, and what is the lookup order between the per-account config dir and the OS keystore?
notes: readClaudeCredentials tries account.configDir/.credentials.json first, then falls back to the macOS Keychain item named 'Claude Code-credentials' (via `security find-generic-password`). The access token is parsed from json.claudeAiOauth.accessToken (with optional expiresAt). It returns null (degrade to none) rather than throwing, and the token is only used to mint an Authorization header — never logged.

task: For reading Codex ChatGPT credentials: which file under the account's config dir holds them, which field is the access token, and where does the ChatGPT account id come from when tokens.account_id is missing?
notes: readCodexCredentials reads configDir/auth.json (the per-account CODEX_HOME), takes tokens.access_token, and uses tokens.account_id when present. When it's missing, it decodes the tokens.id_token JWT and reads the chatgpt_account_id claim under the 'https://api.openai.com/auth' claim object. Returns null when no credential or an API-key-only account.

task: The Codex credential reader pulls chatgpt_account_id out of the id_token via decodeJwtClaims. Does that helper verify the JWT signature before trusting the claim, and how does it actually extract the payload? I want to be sure we're not trusting an unsigned token for something security-sensitive.
notes: A correct answer must state decodeJwtClaims does NOT verify the signature — intentionally. It splits the token on '.', base64url-decodes the MIDDLE (second) segment (replacing - with + and _ with /), and JSON.parses it; it returns an empty object {} for anything that isn't a decodable JWT. This is safe because the token already authenticated the user with the provider and only a non-secret routing claim (the account id) is read — no secret/keypair is needed. It must NOT claim it validates the signature or requires a public key.

task: OpenAI's reset headers are Go-style durations like '1h2m3s', '800ms', or a plain '30'. What unit does parseDurationSeconds return, and what does it return for input it can't parse (so a caller can omit a reset rather than render a wrong one)?
notes: parseDurationSeconds returns a number of SECONDS, summing h/m/s/ms/us/ns components (and accepting a bare numeric string as seconds). For unparseable or partially-matching input it returns undefined (not null, not NaN, never throws), so callers omit resetsAt instead of rendering a wrong time.

task: `smithers usage` seems to remember results between invocations. Where is that cache stored on disk, what happens when the file is missing or corrupt, and is it the same store as the run database?
notes: The usage cache is usage-cache.json living next to accounts.json under the Smithers accounts root (so it honors SMITHERS_HOME). readUsageCache returns an empty { version: 1, entries: {} } when the file is missing or malformed (a cold cache is normal, not an error). writeUsageCache writes atomically via a temp file + rename with mode 0600. It is plain JSON, NOT the SQLite run DB (smithers.db).

task: I ran `smithers usage` twice within ~30 seconds while I have a claude-code, a codex, and an anthropic-api account registered. The second run came back instantly and marked the claude row stale, and even `--fresh` refused to re-probe claude immediately. Walk me through the cache/refresh policy: the per-provider soft interval, the special claude floor, what `--fresh` actually bypasses, and why running all three probes in parallel doesn't corrupt the cache file.
notes: A correct answer must cover: getUsageForAccounts reads the cache ONCE, decides per account, probes the misses in PARALLEL, then writes ONCE — so parallel probes never race the file. Soft refresh intervals are per provider (claude-code 180s, codex 60s, default 30s): within that age a cached report is reused on a normal run and returned with stale: true. `--fresh` bypasses the soft interval but NOT the hard floor of 180s for claude-code, which is enforced unconditionally because the Claude usage endpoint 429s aggressively below 180s. It must NOT claim --fresh forces an immediate claude re-probe.

task: I want to reproduce the `smithers usage` human-readable table in my own tool. Which columns does the table formatter emit, and what does it print when there are no accounts registered?
canonicalAnswer: ACCOUNT, PROVIDER, PLAN, WINDOW, USED, RESETS IN
notes: formatUsageReports renders an aligned table with header columns ACCOUNT, PROVIDER, PLAN, WINDOW, USED, RESETS IN (one row per window; accounts with no windows show a single row with the error/'not supported' note). With zero reports it returns 'No accounts registered. Add one with `smithers agents add`.' It is a quota/rate-limit table, not a token-cost table — there is no COST column. The CLI writes it to stderr while the JSON envelope goes to stdout.

task: In the usage table the 'RESETS IN' column shows things like '2h 41m' or 'now'. Which two helpers turn an ISO resetsAt into that string, and what is shown for a window that has no resetsAt or whose reset is already in the past?
notes: formatRelativeReset(resetsAt, nowMs) returns '' when there is no/invalid timestamp and otherwise delegates to humanizeDurationShort((ms - nowMs)/1000), which renders '5d 3h' / '2h 41m' / '42s' and returns 'now' for any non-positive (past) duration. These are hand-rolled helpers, not date-fns/moment.

task: Author a minimal Smithers workflow. First, a single deterministic compute <Task> (NO agent) imports the smithers usage helpers and, given a captured OpenAI reset header value of '1h30m0s', converts it to seconds and to a short human 'resets in' string, returning both on its typed output. Then a downstream agent <Task> reads that output and drafts a one-line Slack notice telling the team when the rate limit clears. Use the real exported helper functions, not invented ones.
notes: A correct graph is a <Workflow> (with <Sequence>) containing a compute <Task> whose children is a function returning the Row deterministically (no agent=) — it should call parseDurationSeconds('1h30m0s') and humanizeDurationShort(seconds) imported from @smithers-orchestrator/usage (or the package's exports) — followed by an agent <Task> that consumes the compute output. It must NOT invent helpers like parseGoDuration/ctx.usage.

task: Author a Smithers 'pre-deploy quota guard' workflow. Step 1: a deterministic compute <Task> (no agent) gathers usage for every registered account and renders the human table — it should list accounts, fetch their usage through the cached multi-account helper, and format the report. Step 2: route on the result — if any subscription 'percent' window is at/over 90% used, require a human <Approval> before continuing; otherwise proceed. Step 3: an agent <Task> runs the deploy only after the gate clears. Use the real usage package exports.
notes: A correct graph: <Workflow> + <Sequence> with (1) a compute <Task> calling getUsageForAccounts(listAccounts()) and formatUsageReports(...) from @smithers-orchestrator/usage and @smithers-orchestrator/accounts; (2) an <Approval> gate that is reached when a window's usedPercent >= 90; (3) a downstream agent <Task> for the deploy gated on the approval. Must render via `smithers graph`. Must NOT use invented APIs (ctx.getUsage / smithers.usage()) or any mock backend.
